uh, local government was established to meet the needs
of the communities that they serve.
These needs are broad brush
and summed up in the four pillars of wellbeing.
Social, economic, environmental, and cultural.
Local government has never ever been just about
roads rates in rubbish.
Local councils are expected by communities
and by legislation to provide
and support a wealth of services
and facilities from urban regeneration,

local roads, sports fields,
and community hubs, events, and many more.
All of these come under the four wellbeings.
They, they're four pillars providing a platform for livable,
vibrant, productive, flourishing communities.
Removing these pillars from legislation may end up limiting
local government's ability to meet these and other needs.
Councils have robust consultation processes
that ensure communities' voices
are heard when setting plans, priorities, and budgets.
And local communities want more control over their
destinies, not less.
If the four wellbeings are removed,
council may no longer have the legislative backing

In addition, if we strip away the wellbeings,
we risk turning councils into service contractors focused on
cost alone, not on creating places where people belong,

with only 5,500 rate payers, yet we host one
of the highest visitor to rate pair ratios in New Zealand,
um, using the current MB data.
Um, it's 131 visitors to every one resident.
The national average is really, um, seven to one,
and even Queenstown sits at 85 to one.
Um, the problem is that many
of these visitors are day trippers who drive
through from Christchurch through to Queenstown, um,
and they drive through.
They use our infrastructure, which are roads, public, um,
toilets, um, wastewater systems, um, systems,
but they contribute very little
economically to our district.
Um, this place is a immense pressure, um,
on our small district
and our council to be able to deliver services
and funding, um, especially for mixed use infrastructure.
Um, our Lake
Tepa wastewater treatment plant requires a significant
capital investment, a cost that would drive debt upwards,
and a cost that is onerous for a small community.
Um, we've taken steps in this year to actually start
to help ourselves, um, to really target some
of the high tourism spots
and to really make sure that we do, uh,
extract some value from the, um, the drive through tourist
by, um, having a user pay toilet system in place.
Um, so we urge you to consider that context.
Um, in the light of some of the, um, the purpose definitions
and the core services definitions, um, we are convinced
or we are concerned that the bill narrows the scope
of local government, removing the forward wellbeings
and defining core services to ri to rigidly risk,
sidelining vital functions like tourism, housing
and economic development,
and even community development as well.
Section ten's wording on economic
development is very narrow.
And Section 11 A is incomplete and rigid.
We do believe that the council should retain flexibility
to be able to support local priorities.
Investors invest in drivers of local growth, um,
as they are related to tourism
and as they are related to supporting, um,
growth in our districts.
That means being able to support housing initiatives.
Um, we'd also like to be able to partner meaningfully
with central government through regional deals,
and we'd also like to, um, make sure that we can contribute,
um, and continue to invest in tourism
and tourism related infrastructure so
that we can support the re the regional
tourism, um, growth map.
Um, as highlighted by Minister Upton, drivers
of growth locally mean that we need
to invest in affordable housing initiatives
to drive enough housing, uh, for the workers
that work in various areas.
For example, in tepo, uh, housing, the Airbnb ratio,
um, that is there in Tepo is around 50% of the houses
that are there are actually just Airbnbs.
That that does not include, um, other commercial enterprises
and other, and other commercial, um, operations.
Um, that reduces significantly the amount of housing
that is available for, uh, workers.
Um, we also believe that we do need to continue.
We are in a unique environment
and we do need to continue
to support environmental stewardship.
That means being able to work alongside our local trusts
and to be able to look at contributing into initiatives
that address wilding pine risks
and also initiatives that target pest control
and maintain, um, our local biodiversity.
Um, we would like to see the words provide more scope
for placemaking investment that creates a better environment
for locals and
consequently visitors through the development
of mixed use infrastructure.
There is a risk that as the bill progresses, um,
that the words would be tightened
to close down the tourism investment altogether.
And for us, being able to invest in our RTO,
which does a lot of significant marketing
and, um, contributes towards a tourism growth,
um, that is vital.
Um, moving on to benchmarking and performance.
Bench benchmarking, we believe needs
to reflect local context without it when, uh,
right when people are out there comparing, um, comparisons
between districts, it's misleading
and, um, it risks distorting their decision making.
For districts like ours, seasonal tourism surges
and geographic isolations matter
potentially when they then compare those stats from the
different districts, um, um, that could lead
to inaccurate pictures of efficiency and effectiveness.
Effectiveness, right.
Capping while not legislated, the concept is concerning.
The New South Wales experience with rate capping offers.
A clear warning councils in New Zealand have faced
in New South Wales, have faced deferred maintenance under
investment and infrastructure
and increased debt due to fixed rate caps.
The policy failed to account for inflation population growth
and rising service costs, forcing councils to cut services
and to rely on federal grants.
It also undermined local decision making,
removing flexibility to respond to community needs.
In McKenzie, a blanket cap would be especially damaging.
Our costs are driven by tourism,
the unique alpine conditions
and infrastructure demands that don't fit a one size
or a one size fits all model.
For example, I'll talk about our roading in winter.
We have significant weather events, um,
and we, our road payers, um, have
to fund those snow clearing events, um, largely
because the funding has been cut, uh, from NZTA, um,
also road safety.
We see significant, um, I impacts
of poor tourist driving in winter
and we are having to deal with, um,
supporting emergency responses, um, to be able to deal
to accidents on our roads erodes, um,
regulatory relief and governance.
We support efforts to reduce, um,
outdated compliance burdens,
but we oppose removing to Kang Maori considerations
and CCO appointments relief must uphold t obligations.
We also support extending CCEO terms
for continuity and stability.
In closing, we asked the committee
to preserve local flexibility, avoid narrowing council roles
that would restrict investments in economic development
and tourism reject rates capping based on the clear evidence
from New South Wales
and to support collaborative funding for tourism
and infrastructure, uh, with central governments.
Um, I welcome your questions, Karen.
I just might ask if you would like
to contribute to anything.
So look, I, I support everything that Angela has said.
We have a unique climate and district in the McKenzie.
Um, and it, it isn't, um, an easy place for people
to live, uh, but they want to be here.
Uh, on top of that, the volume of tourists that we, uh, have
through our district, both those staying
and those also passing through, um, they're here
because of the unique environment that we have.
Um, but they do put pressure.
We are expecting, um, with China, recognizing
that they're coming back this summer,
that we will be even further under pressure
as we go into the summer season.
Uh, and if we look at our tar rating in relation to the rest
of New Zealand, um, the average tar rating
for New Zealand is 49, as is 11.
So while our communities, uh, recognize the benefit
that tourism gives, um, they don't want
to be in such a position that they're having to bear the,
the full cost of that.
So we want government to work with us
and have place-based funding, um, to be able to help us
to deliver the goals that our communities want,
but also the goals that, um, the, uh, the, the country
as a whole wants in terms of, um, GDP growth, et cetera.
So, um, all of the points that Angela has has made there,
um, that is, that is the collective response
that's come from our council at large.
Uh, and we would like to, um, to do what we can
and advocate for the Mackenzie, uh, which is why we said
that we would speak at this,
at this select committee hearing.
Uh, thank you Angela and Carol.
We've actually run out of time. We've gone over, actually.
So thank you for joining us today.
No problem. Thank you very much. Do,
Do we have, are we, are we getting this person back?
Community justice? So they've
decided they're an hour behind.
Yeah. So, well, I think we just break. Yeah.
Basically say that they're not here.
So reconvene just before one?
Uh, yeah, if we could,
um, 10 minute break.
